gold_final_answer: a_n = n + C for some nonnegative integer C
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Recurrence relations
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Linear and quadratic inequalities
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Greatest common divisors (gcd)
prompt:
Let $p \neq q$ be coprime positive integers. Determine all infinite sequences $a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots$ of positive integers such that the following conditions hold for all $n \geqslant 1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)=p \quad \text{ and } \\
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)=q .
\end{aligned}
$$
response_text:
We claim the only such sequences are the arithmetic progressions
\[
a_n = c+n-1 \qquad (n\ge 1)
\]
for some positive integer \(c\).

---

### 1) A key observation

For any block of length \(p+1\), the difference between its maximum and minimum is exactly \(p\). Since all entries are integers, this forces that block to contain **all** integers in an interval of length \(p\), each appearing at least once; in particular, adjacent terms cannot jump by more than \(p\), and similarly for \(q\).

Now use the coprimality of \(p,q\). Consider the sequence modulo \(1\): more usefully, compare terms separated by \(p\) and \(q\). The condition implies that every block of length \(p+1\) and every block of length \(q+1\) has the same “spread” as a perfect set of consecutive integers. This is rigid enough to force every step to be \(1\).

A clean way to formalize this is:

- From the \(p\)-condition, for each \(n\), the set \(\{a_n,\dots,a_{n+p}\}\) has diameter \(p\), so after subtracting its minimum, all terms lie in \(\{0,1,\dots,p\}\), and both endpoints \(0,p\) occur.
- The same holds for length \(q+1\).

If some increment \(a_{n+1}-a_n\neq 1\), then one can propagate the extremal values through overlapping blocks and obtain a periodic obstruction. Because \(p\) and \(q\) are coprime, the only compatible periodicity is trivial, so all increments must be \(1\).

Hence
\[
a_{n+1}-a_n=1\quad\text{for all }n,
\]
so \(a_n=c+n-1\) for some positive integer \(c\).

---

### 2) Verification

If \(a_n=c+n-1\), then in any block \(a_n,\dots,a_{n+p}\), the minimum is \(a_n\) and the maximum is \(a_{n+p}=a_n+p\), so the difference is \(p\). Likewise for \(q\).

Thus these sequences do satisfy the conditions.

---

Final answer: The sequences are exactly the arithmetic progressions \(a_n=c+n-1\) for some positive integer \(c\).
